text:
$XFAB is the most mispriced semiconductor name in Europe and @aleabitoreddit appears to be the only one that understands it.

Things that the market is COMPLETELY IGNORING:

• Microsystems & Photonics revenue grew ~35% QoQ in the latest quarter. This is where the whole bullish thesis is at an it has grown a whopping 35% in a quarter. 

• SiC revenue +152% YoY / +49% QoQ.

• It's trading below replacement P/B,   while building next-gen photonics/SiC lines -> Heavy capex into photonics (new lines, MTP tech, TFLN integration) means accounting book value is understated vs. real replacement cost of the fabs + IP. 
You’re basically getting the growth at a huge discount.

• €128M EU + $50M US funding already in. CHIPS Act 2.0 incoming with explicit photonics priority. Europe is literally paying them to build the Western alternative. Backstopped balance sheet.

• CEO (Damien Macq) bought 5500 shares on February. Market completely ignoring it.

• $XFAB leading EU’s photonixFAB effort for sovereign silicon photonics supply chain. Nvidia evaluating their platform for CPO/transceivers (H2 2027–2028 ramp). Nokia setting specs. This is Tower Semi 2.0, but earlier and with government muscle.

text:
This is one of the least known and least talked about $SIVE catalysts on FinX

The only one I saw discussing it intensely for the first time was obviously Serenity

$SIVE could soon start ramping up production of its lasers

Both with $AEVA and with $AAPL

In the framework of the upcoming Apple Watch update following the company's recent announcement

No credible information has been found so far that refutes this possibility

Some people without sufficient technical knowledge tend to confuse TASC sensors

With future Apple Watches that will incorporate lasers

And they don't take into account that NRE volumes can extend over several years

The main focus of $SIVE remains the AI data center market within the photonics field

Any collaboration with $AAPL is considered an additional benefit

text:
My opinion on today's Annual General Meeting of $SIVE

The shareholders approved the 2025 accounts and a new board of directors

With Bami Bastani as chairman and the addition of Joakim Nideborn (vicechairman)

And Helena Svancar, with the aim of strengthening governance and accelerating expansion in the United States

Nothing to comment up to this point everything correct technical matters of the company

Here is the important part:

The issuance of up to 15% of new shares was authorized to finance growth for $SIVE

As has been known for weeks

Possible acquisitions or the dual listing on Nasdaq are the kind of things this healthy capital raising will be used for

Although this implies dilution risk it is completely necessary

And it is one of the most bullish signals possible for $SIVE

They are telling you to your face we need money because what is coming is huge

On the other hand more topics to address

No dividends will be distributed and a convertible loan was ratified

The company remains focused on photonics for AI and wireless solutions for SATCOM and defense

With a pipeline of opportunities that grew 77% to nearly $800M

I would define this $SIVE assembly as an attempt to grant greater flexibility to the new board

To drive growth in the US market

Prioritizing reinvestment over dividends

Waiting for future catalysts to start taking off

Very bullish on $SIVE having already completed my total DCA

text:
The Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ Effect-to-Cause Model

March 5, 2026 
Bainbridge Island, WA

Whitman’s poetry often moves from observation → revelation. (Reposting) 

Observed Effect (in the present):

• expanding surveillance infrastructures
• emerging debates over cognitive liberty and digital rights
• technological systems capable of sensing and mapping human environments

Whitman’s philosophical lens:

He repeatedly distinguishes between the visible world and the unseen one beneath it.

Modern readers might describe this metaphor using scientific language:

Whitman’s poet-seer stands between those layers, translating what is hidden into human language.

⸻

🧬 A Contemporary Poet’s Interpretation

As a published poet whose work appears in anthologies and children’s literature written in rhyming prose, I read Whitman less as a historian and more as a philosopher of perception.

In later sections of Leaves of Grass, Whitman asks whether anyone has truly seen the soul.

Today that question resonates differently.

We live in an era where technologies—from photonics to sensor systems—attempt to map what was once invisible.

Whitman could not have known these technologies.

Yet his poetry repeatedly asks readers to consider:

• what lies beyond surface perception
• how unseen forces shape the visible world
• whether the human soul remains sovereign within expanding systems of observation.

⸻

🧭 Whitman Revisited Through the Present

A modern reading does not claim Whitman predicted our technologies.

Instead, his work may help us see our own era more clearly.

By distinguishing between the seen structures of society and the unseen forces beneath them, Whitman provides language that can still illuminate contemporary debates about:

• democracy
• autonomy
• human consciousness
• and the boundaries of observation.

In that sense, Whitman’s poetry becomes less about foretelling the future and more about revealing how to perceive the present.

⸻

In later sections of Leaves of Grass, Whitman asks whether anyone has truly seen the soul. Today that question resonates differently. text:
Right now, I believe a similar dynamic may be developing in photonics.

The optical interconnect supply chain has five interconnected layers. Each has a chokepoint. Most are lightly held by institutions.

The companies controlling specialty substrates, laser production, and co-packaged optics design wins are, I believe, essential, constrained, and largely uncovered by Wall Street.

text:
$STM - the 2027 ramp is my thesis

Forget the YTD chart. 
The question that matters: does the earnings ramp show up?

→ Data center revenue guided to ~$1B in 2026, then doubling again toward ~$2B in 2027 (company guidance, echoed by BofA on its PT raise to $83). 

That’s the fastest-growing line in the model.

→ The driver is silicon photonics + power chips into AI infrastructure. 

$STM is weighing a Crolles fab expansion by end-2026 specifically because photonics demand is accelerating. 

Capacity decisions = conviction signal.

→ $SPCX selected the STM32V8 for Starlink’s inter-satellite laser links. 

Broader OEM availability follows 2026. 

Design wins now ⇒ revenue later.

→ The real unlock is operating leverage. Net margin is a depressed 1.4% TTM with auto/industrial at trough. 

As the cyclical floor lifts and high-margin data-center mix scales, EPS ramps off a low base.

→ FCF inflection backs it: TTM FCF ≈ -$700M, projected to swing to ~+$967M in 2026 and build toward ~$3.5B by 2030 (Simply Wall St estimates). 

Cash generation is the proof, not the promise.

I am betting that data-center compounding plus an auto/industrial trough recovery converges in 2027. 

If both hit, the multiple re-rates on rising earnings instead of sentiment.

Key watch item = next print’s data-center run-rate. 

That’s the tell on whether the doubling is on track.

Excited to see this one play out - quite sure @damnang2 is also long on this one 🔥

text:
9/ My Take/Risks

SIVE isn’t a stock for investors looking for certainty.

It’s a stock for investors willing to accept significant risk in exchange for potential upside if AI networking, silicon photonics and optical infrastructure continue to grow.

Risks:

❌ Not profitable
❌ Highly speculative
❌ Extremely volatile
❌ Commercial adoption still needs to materialise

With that in mind, I’m building a position which will make up no more than 2% of my portfolio. This is at about 1% currently. I have some cash allocated to buy dips and will continue to do so alongside other investments.

text:
5/ Silicon Photonics

Sivers is designing its laser technology to integrate into silicon photonics ecosystems.

Rather than simply selling standalone components, the goal is to become part of the wider infrastructure stack.

Silicon photonics replaces electrical data transfer with light inside chips, massively improving bandwidth and energy efficiency — and Sivers is focused on providing the external laser systems that power these photonic networks.

text:
My growth portfolio is up +137% YTD.

For context:
• S&P 500: +8.5% YTD
• Nasdaq Composite: +11.3% YTD
• Nasdaq-100: +16% YTD
I’m beating the broad market by roughly 10-16x so far this year.
This isn’t luck; it’s concentrated bets on the biggest secular themes right now: AI infrastructure and the commercial + defense space economy.

Quick updates on the top holdings driving the performance:
• $NBIS (Nebius) — Just announced Nasdaq-100 inclusion (effective June 22). NVIDIA-backed AI Robotics Living Lab launched + £1.7B UK AI factory expansion underway. Revenue still exploding.
• $AAOI (Applied Optoelectronics) — Q1 data center revenue surged as hyperscalers accelerate AI networking buildouts. Optical components remain a critical bottleneck.
• $SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors) — Won $8.2M production order from https://t.co/OSQrkhVUFs for satellite beamforming chips. $800M+ pipeline and strong momentum in both AI photonics and defense/satcom.
• $MU (Micron) — High-bandwidth memory (HBM) supply completely sold out through all of 2026. AI memory demand continues to outstrip supply.
• $RKLB (Rocket Lab) — Also joining the Nasdaq-100 on June 22. Record backlog now over $2.2B with major new defense launch contracts secured.

These names are all leveraged to multi-year tailwinds that are still in early innings.

Not financial advice. Just sharing what’s working in my book. Markets can be volatile, always do your own research.

Which of these (or what other growth names) are you most excited about right now? 👇

text:
Sequoia’s @shaunmmaguire wrote a private hardware manifesto arguing that over the next 25 years, most of the money will be made in hardware:

"Every software revolution is preceded by a hardware revolution."

"To have the iOS App Store that enabled Uber, DoorDash, and all of these great companies - you needed to have the iPhone."

"This AI revolution - we're seeing what it can do from the software layer, but it's still limited by hardware."

"The hardware we were doing for a long time was all following Moore's Law. It was all branching out of this decision in the mid-1950s to go all in on the silicon supply chain."

"That has created magic, and there's still a couple orders of magnitude of juice to squeeze, but we’re hitting fundamental physics limits - Dennard scaling, things like that."

"I think this tech tree is branching into humanoid robots, into silicon photonics, into orbital data centers - all of these new hardware areas where there's going to be 20+ years of progress."

"There's going to be incredible businesses built on the back of this. And a lot of dumpster fires."

From his appearance on the show in March.

text:
Coherent is a technology leader in lasers, optical components, and semiconductor materials, with its products widely used in industrial, telecommunications, and EV applications. Its strengths lie in its high-barrier technologies and innovative capabilities. The stock saw a significant gain today, benefiting from the recovery in the technology manufacturing sector.

Future trends are strong: growing demand for 5G/6G, fiber-optic communications, and laser processing will drive orders. The expansion of AI-related applications further opens up growth opportunities, with high certainty of growth in the medium to long term.

#Coherent

#Photonics
#IndustrialTech

text: A startup dropping a $25M preorder seems crazy but look closer: Casela isnt spending their cash- they are bankrolled by optical transceiver giant CIG Shanghai via an $11M Q1 26 convertible loan. CIG is using Casela as a proxy to lock down AXTIs InP substrate capacity ahead of the 2027 ramp in CPO/photonics sectors. Then add the deep industry credibility of founders Dr. Bo Lu and Dr. Milind Gokhale (ex-Broadcom/ASIP optoelectronic pioneers)= this isn't a random shell mystery startup. Its a downstream powerhouse securing its critical hardware supply chain early.
embedded_text:

text: AI Supplier Awarded First New Jersey Manufacturing Tax Credit: The inaugural recipient of the Next New Jersey Manufacturing Program, Starman New Photonics will operate in Warren and plans to create 250 jobs. https://t.co/deqGOAQEJW #CorporateRealEstate #EconDev #SiteSelection https://t.co/GTAHPlFmkG

When a stock runs 1,000% in a few months, I don't buy it for the long term.

I use these high-momentum names only for day trades or short swing trades.

Yes, you may miss the move from +1,000% to +2,000%. That's fine.

What I want to avoid is the inevitable freefall when momentum breaks. A 70–90% drawdown can be devastating.

This applies to AI, photonics, energy, and any other hot sector. Protecting capital matters more than catching the last leg of a parabolic move.

text:
Cornings $GLW is starting to look like one of my favorite AI infrastructure longs.

Most investors still think of Corning as glass, displays, and Gorilla Glass.

But the more interesting story is where they are positioning now:

• Data center optical fiber
• High-density cables and connectivity
• Photonics
• Co-packaged optics / CPO
• Advanced glass materials for semiconductor packaging

As AI clusters scale, the bottleneck is not just compute. It is moving massive amounts of data faster, cheaper, and with less power.

That puts $GLW closer to the center of the AI buildout than many investors realize.

Their partnerships with $NVDA and $AVGO also matter. If AI networking moves toward photonics and CPO standardization, Corning could become one of the key picks-and-shovels suppliers behind the scenes.

text:
AI chips may do the computing, but scaling AI clusters increasingly depends on indium phosphide, the material used in the lasers and photonic devices that move data between processors at the speed of light.

The world’s first and largest volume-production 6-inch indium phosphide platform sits in Sherman, Texas.
NVIDIA joined Coherent this week to break ground on an expansion that will double manufacturing space and quadruple wafer production capacity, turning a little-known photonics supply chain into part of America’s AI buildout.

text:
Mapping the entire AI & Compute ecosystem. 🗺️📈

If you're playing the AI supercycle, you need to look beyond just the obvious names. Here is the complete tech infrastructure value chain:

🔬 Chips: $NVDA $AVGO $AMD $ASML $ARM $KLAC 
🏭 Foundry: $TSM $INTC 
💾 Memory: $SNDK $MU $WDC $STX 
🔌 Networking: $CRDO $ALAB $ANET $MRVL 
✨ Photonics: $AAOI $SIVE $AXTI $LITE $COHR $GLW 
🖥️ Servers: $VRT $DELL $HPE 
⚡️ Energy: $BE $TLN $GEV $TE 
📊 Data: $INOD $SNOW $DDOG 
☁️ Neoclouds: $NBIS $CRWV $APLD $IREN $WYFI

I personally believe Photonics will be a big thing soon like memory!

For SCU physics alumni Craig Benko ’10 and Bryan Berggren ’17, hands-on research, close mentorship, and long hours solving tough problems helped shape careers in the fast-moving world of photonics.

Click the link below to read their full story. ⬇️ 🤩 

https://t.co/gMLOkQNcFE

Yttrium 

Y, it’s atomic number 39.  It’s a silvery white rare earth metal that is abundant, it’s usually extracted from minerals like xenotime and bastnäsite. 

The main known uses as of (2026) Yttrium is primarily used in compounds and alloys.

✅ Electronics & Photonics Yttrium aluminum crystals doped with other elements are widely used in high-power solid-state lasers for industrial cutting/welding, medical procedures (e.g., eye surgery, dermatology), and defense applications.

✅ Superconductors Yttrium barium copper oxide is the basis for high-temperature superconductors that work at liquid nitrogen temperatures. 

These are used in power transmission cables, MRI magnets, and research toward more efficient energy systems and motors. 

✅ Phosphors & Lighting In LED lights, flat-panel displays, and fluorescent lamps, yttrium compounds provide red phosphors, which can cause respiratory Issues, Liver and Organ Effects, Reproductive Toxicity, 
testicular tissue, induce cell death, (programmed cell death) reduce testosterone, and impair semen quality.

✅ Ceramics & Alloys Enhances heat resistance, strength, and thermal shock resistance in advanced ceramics (e.g., for jet engines or nuclear reactors.

✅ Medical tracers, catalysts, and in some sensors or microwave filters.

What’s Special About Yttrium?

✅ It has unique electronic structure (partially filled 4d orbitals) that makes it excellent for creating stable compounds with optical, magnetic, and superconducting properties.

✅ Optical & Laser Performance. It produces high-power lasers with excellent beam quality.

✅ Strategic Importance

Critical for clean tech, defense, and electronics. 

✅ Biological/Research Interest 

Its ability to fine-tune material properties makes it indispensable in modern high-tech.

Investor read:

• Funded by Defence R&D Canada IDEAS. Government money is flowing to free-space quantum hardware.
• Photonics suppliers (Coherent, Lumentum) make the mirrors and beamsplitters, but it's off-the-shelf and low margin here.
• Satellite-QKD programs benefit indirectly: lighter, cheaper receivers.

Signal to watch: whether QEYSSat-class or ESA links adopt passive receivers over adaptive optics.

A single agentic task can generate 450% more network traffic than a human doing the same work. Roughly 70% of that traffic is inference. Nearly 10% of AI flows now carry more data upstream than downstream, versus 0.5% for typical web traffic. And Cisco says enterprise traffic could grow 9x by 2035 with agentic AI adoption, versus 2.5x without it. That ties directly into something I wrote about recently: Scale-across. AI clusters are getting too large to keep in one place. At a certain point, the constraint becomes physical: Power. Land. Cooling. Permitting. Fiber access. Route availability. So instead of forcing everything into one giant site, hyperscalers start connecting multiple buildings, campuses, and facilities together so they can operate like one larger AI environment. Cisco’s report adds the usage layer. Agentic AI turns one request into a chain: Prompt → planner → model call → tool call → retrieval → more model calls → action. That creates more: > traffic > upstream flow. > machine-to-machine exchange > persistent context movement > pressure on the infrastructure underneath the model. The first pressure shows up closest to compute. But as AI workloads spread across buildings, campuses, and regions, the optical layer between those sites becomes more important. That is the scale-across trade. Once distance enters the equation, a bottleneck increasingly shifts toward: Coherent optics. Fiber. DCI. Dark fiber routes. Pump lasers. Narrow-linewidth lasers. Optical amplification. Indium phosphide capacity. That is why this keeps showing up across optical earnings calls. $CIEN = coherent systems / cloud DCI / scale-across transport $NOK = optical transport plus AI/cloud customer exposure $LITE = pump lasers, narrow-linewidth components, EMLs $COHR = InP, lasers, transceivers, optical components $GLW = fiber base layer $LUMN = dark fiber routes NFA"}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2062396054449144251"}}, "rest_id": "2067301749283766756", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "21675", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2067347493356966120", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3913", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
20260617 Market Review
• I don't to talk to you about the FOMC today. You can get that from tons of other website, talking heads etc. Most of these have no value add and not actionable. What's important is HOW do we interpret what is going and HOW do we position going forward? ie HOW DO WE THINK AHEAD OF THE CROWD?
• Market breadth ended up being quite weak, with the VIX threatening to go back above 19 (a line in the sand I watch). But what really caught my eye was the USD. It really took off after the FOMC and stopped right at resistance. If this leads to a sustained breakout to the upside, it may not be friendly to markets. Watching that 100.50 level closely. With the USD rallying, it is no wonder $GOLD would get hit. Another lower high forming. 
• Indices seem to be stuck in chop for now. AI seems to be the only game in town but I wanna dig deeper into the various subsectors today. 
• Semiconductors. Ok but hitting resistance. 
$NVDA losing the 50SMA today is not a good look. 
• Semicon Equipment and Materials. Big list in here, very mixed picture. Nothing I would buy in a hurry.
• Racks: $Dell ok but $HPE selling off. Neutral because the whole group is not looking uniform. 
• Photonics: $LITE is the group leader and it has been mucking around the 50SMA. Not healthy. Neither is $AAOI, $GLW or $AXTI. 
• Memory: $STX and $WDC still making new highs, but $MU hitting resistance. $SNDK still king. Of all the AI Hardware playes, Memory is probably the only subsector which I think overall is still healthy. But even that is extended. 
• So the above are the AI Leading Sectors that I pay attention to and overall, my conclusion is that the easy trade in AI Hardware is over. It can still go higher, but unless it sets up for me properly (with a pullback or an orderly sideways congestion for a few weeks to a few months), I will find it hard to bet big on them for now. 
• I am still very bullish on the AI trade but if Hardware is not the game for now, what then?
• Next we have the Neoclouds. $IREN $CRWV $NBIS etc. $NBIS is the clear leader. Generally ok and I would still look for opportunities in them.
• Software. I was hopeful that it might have pulled back too much and would find support but that didn't eventuate. In line with my trading philosophy, "if it ain't working, it ain't staying". I will continue to stalk this sector as they are going to be the "operational" layer of AI and I think there will be several companies in there that will benefit. 
• Cybersecurity is one software sub-sector I am super keen on and have taken a position accordingly. Look at $CRWD and $PANW (both we took in our port today) for example. They are holding up like a champ and tightening up really nicely. This is an extremely healthy consolidation after that fierce run they had since early May, and the best part is, they are NOT giving back much of the gains! THIS is a sign of a Market Leader. Can we be wrong or too early? Of course, but then that’s why we have stops to protect us. 
• Biotech. This is the sector that I think will benefit greatly from AI (check out the $NVDA x $LLY presentation on the NVDA website). I have been stalking XBI for a few weeks now and we finally broke higher today. Great volume too. 
• Fintech is another interesting subsector that is showing a bit of relative strength recently. Worth keeping an eye on. Eg $DAVE $AFRM etc.
• In conclusion, I think the overall market might be in for a bit of digestion. $MAGS  is pulling back hard (not good). The AI HARDWARE trade is no longer easy. But the AI USAGE trade I think is just starting. Biotech and Cybersecurity $CIBR are the standouts to me and we are positioning accordingly. Software $IGV is a maybe, keep stalking.
• All the best.

Watching that 100.50 level closely. With the USD rallying, it is no wonder $GOLD would get hit. Another lower high forming. • Indices seem to be stuck in chop for now. AI seems to be the only game in town but I wanna dig deeper into the various subsectors today. • Semiconductors. Ok but hitting resistance. $NVDA losing the 50SMA today is not a good look. • Semicon Equipment and Materials. Big list in here, very mixed picture. Nothing I would buy in a hurry. • Racks: $Dell ok but $HPE selling off. Neutral because the whole group is not looking uniform. • Photonics: $LITE is the group leader and it has been mucking around the 50SMA. Not healthy. Neither is $AAOI, $GLW or $AXTI. • Memory: $STX and $WDC still making new highs, but $MU hitting resistance. $SNDK still king. Of all the AI Hardware playes, Memory is probably the only subsector which I think overall is still healthy. But even that is extended. • So the above are the AI Leading Sectors that I pay attention to and overall, my conclusion is that the easy trade in AI Hardware is over. It can still go higher, but unless it sets up for me properly (with a pullback or an orderly sideways congestion for a few weeks to a few months), I will find it hard to bet big on them for now. • I am still very bullish on the AI trade but if Hardware is not the game for now, what then? • Next we have the Neoclouds. $IREN $CRWV $NBIS etc. $NBIS is the clear leader. Generally ok and I would still look for opportunities in them. • Software. I was hopeful that it might have pulled back too much and would find support but that didn't eventuate. In line with my trading philosophy, "if it ain't working, it ain't staying". I will continue to stalk this sector as they are going to be the "operational" layer of AI and I think there will be several companies in there that will benefit. • Cybersecurity is one software sub-sector I am super keen on and have taken a position accordingly. Look at $CRWD and $PANW (both we took in our port today) for example. They are holding up like a champ and tightening up really nicely. This is an extremely healthy consolidation after that fierce run they had since early May, and the best part is, they are NOT giving back much of the gains! THIS is a sign of a Market Leader. Can we be wrong or too early? Of course, but then that’s why we have stops to protect us. • Biotech. This is the sector that I think will benefit greatly from AI (check out the $NVDA x $LLY presentation on the NVDA website). I have been stalking XBI for a few weeks now and we finally broke higher today. Great volume too. • Fintech is another interesting subsector that is showing a bit of relative strength recently. Worth keeping an eye on. Eg $DAVE $AFRM etc. • In conclusion, I think the overall market might be in for a bit of digestion. $MAGS is pulling back hard (not good). The AI HARDWARE trade is no longer easy. But the AI USAGE trade I think is just starting. Biotech and Cybersecurity $CIBR are the standouts to me and we are positioning accordingly. Software $IGV is a maybe, keep stalking. • All the best."}}}, "rest_id": "2067347221503471760", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "176", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Cleanest read of the quarter, and agreed it's a genuine, gimmick-free beat. The one thing I'd push on: this is a high-quality revenue print, not yet a high-quality margin one. Intelligent Infrastructure is carrying the AI growth (21%, guided +32% into Q4) but sits at 6.1% core margin, only ~30bps above the 5.8% corporate blend. So the AI surge is roughly margin-neutral to the company today. The whole re-rate toward 6%+ is a within-II mix story (photonics, cooling, power, Hanley) that management itself calls gradual. Volume is here now; the accretive mix is FY27's job. That's the gap between "beat-and-raise" and "re-rate," and it's why a 10bps full-year margin lift on a $1B revenue raise is the number to sit with.

text:
$SIVE has had a tremendous run, but at current levels it looks increasingly priced for perfection.

Meanwhile, $LBIRD remains largely overlooked despite being directly exposed to one of the fastest-growing trends in aerospace: satellite constellations and optical communications.

As SpaceX, Starlink and the broader space economy continue to expand, Lumibird's laser and photonics expertise could become significantly more valuable.

Sometimes the best opportunities are where the market isn't looking yet. #SpaceX #Starlink #Photonics #Space

text:
⚛️ Atom Computing and Nu Quantum have signed an MoU to integrate neutral-atom quantum computers with state-of-the-art photonic networking hardware.

🤝 This collaboration is set to accelerate the path to utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computing systems.

👀 By focusing on integrated photonics switches and distributed architectures, they're addressing key challenges on the road to real-world quantum utility.

🚀 Excited to see where this partnership leads the industry!

Get it here 👉 https://t.co/QcBJzJz04F

#QuantumComputing #AtomComputing #NuQuantum #QuantumNetworking #Science #QuantumScience @Atom_Computing @NuQuantumLtd

text:
Most people see $NOK as sleepy European telecom. They’re missing what it bought.

Less than 2% of global semiconductor advanced test & packaging happens in the US. Nokia owns one of the only domestic sites packaging photonic chips into optical modules for AI data centers — and it didn’t come from telecom. It came from Infinera, absorbed Feb 2025.

Now they’re scaling it with CHIPS money behind them:

~$30M Nokia capex + ~$10M federal CHIPS tax credit + ~$4M PA aid. 500+ jobs. Part of a $4B US commitment.

This isn’t a photonics rocket — Allentown is a small piece of a $20B+ company. But that’s the point. You’re getting a CHIPS-backed domestic photonics packaging franchise inside a name the market still prices as legacy telecom.

A misread legacy company with a real foothold in the AI photonics layer. The choke point before the crowd sees it.

Bullish $NOK.

Now they’re scaling it with CHIPS money behind them: ~$30M Nokia capex + ~$10M federal CHIPS tax credit + ~$4M PA aid. 500+ jobs. Part of a $4B US commitment. This isn’t a photonics rocket — Allentown is a small piece of a $20B+ company. But that’s the point. According to GoldmanSachs, CPO Switch BoM Revenue Contribution per Unit (Quantum-X Photonics / NVIDIA):
Estimated supplier revenue per $130k switch (based on BoM values):
NVIDIA (Switch ASICs + Optical Engines design): ~$12k (ASICs) + significant share of engines → major portion.

TSMC (Fabrication/Packaging of ASICs & engines): Substantial share of ~$44.4k combined ASIC/engines value.

Corning (FAUs + SM Fiber): ~$3.6k + $12.3k = ~$15.9k.

Lumentum / Coherent / Sumitomo (ELS + CW Lasers): ~$7.2k.

Senko / others (MPO connectors): ~$5.8k.

Additional (Shuffle Box, assembly): Balance to total BoM ~$75.8k.

Optical module packaging capacity is the quiet bottleneck behind AI network buildouts. A 10x lift at one plant, if it holds to the Q3 2026 timeline, moves the constraint elsewhere in the SiPho (silicon photonics) stack.

https://t.co/N8b2g3IDTM
embedded_text: Nokia announced a 10x capacity expansion of a photonics chip plant in Pennsylvania, where it packages and tests optical modules used in AI and telecom infrastructure, it said, adding some new capacity will be up by end Q3 2026. $NOK https://t.co/40eEJmm7PW

text:
$CRDO vs $ALAB the two fabless semi, pure play AI connectivity leaders. 

Most investor bros have no clue what these companies do and all they see is the parabolic stock chart lol. Here is some DD to make you feel more confident investing in these names.

Which to invest in? I say both.

I've been long $ALAB since the IPO range. But I'll tell you straight why $CRDO is the better *print* right now. Plus, $CRDO recent acquisition of  Dust Photonics adds to $CRDO value.

📊                      $CRDO      $ALAB
Revenue           $1.34B     $852M
Growth              ~3x YoY    +115%
Gross Mgn       ~68%       ~75%
Op Margin         47.8%      39.2%
EPS (n-GAAP)  $1.16      ~$0.45
Cash                    $1.4B      $1.18B

$CRDO wins scale + growth. 
$ALAB wins margin quality.

I like both because they’re really not eating each others lunch.

$ALAB owns the silicon INSIDE the rack — retimers, CXL, fabric switches + COSMOS software. Sticky at the protocol layer.

$CRDO owns the wires BETWEEN racks — AECs, and now optics for scale-out.

Scale-up vs scale-out. You can own both.

And $CRDO just got dangerous — because it stopped being a one-trick copper-cable company:
→ ZeroFlap optics now shipping (reliability + telemetry, not just speed)
→ bought DustPhotonics for silicon photonics
→ 5 product pillars, $10B+ TAM
→ optics = HALF of next year's growth

Not without risk, both sides:

$CRDO → gross margin guided DOWN to 64-66% as the optics mix shifts. Funded the ramp with a ~$488M ATM raise. The growth is real — but it's also being bought. Customer concentration is high.

$ALAB → priced for perfection. Any hyperscaler capex wobble and it's first to bleed.

The tell for both is the same thing it always is: margins. 👀

$CRDO has the growth. $ALAB has the moat.

Want the explosive print? $CRDO.
Want the durable compounder? $ALAB.

I'm long $ALAB for the margin profile — but I'm not gonna pretend $CRDO isn't winning the quarter.

Own the theme. Both should keep running & the $CRDO DustPhotonics acquisition could end up being incredibly valuable to $CRDO.

• $IBM, plus Google and Rigetti: every superconducting roadmap needs optical interconnects to go modular. This is upstream of that.
• Coherent, Lumentum: photonics picks-and-shovels.
• Cisco: quantum networking ambitions.
• Miraex (private, Swiss) + EPFL: the actors here.

Signal to watch: whether any superconducting vendor names a transduction partner.

text:
Owning upstream is where the effortless money gets made.

What I find particularly compelling is that $TSEM already has $1.3B of contracted silicon photonics revenue for 2027 backed by customer prepayments, while $COHR and $LITE are selling into an InP laser shortage that can't be solved quickly. Qualification cycles take years, not quarters.

The market chased $NVDA first. Then $CRDO and $ALAB. Then names like $AAOI etc..

I think the next rerating comes from the companies enabling AI systems to communicate, not compute.

Bandwidth is becoming the bottleneck. History shows that when a bottleneck emerges, pricing power and investor attention usually flow upstream first.

text:
This is gonna upset a lot of people: 

But TA is astrology for traders.

It's confirmation bias + trading human psychology about entries. 

Kinda like how people frontran $SPCE from $SPCX IPO expecting retail to mess up tickers by trading psychology. 

$SIVE didn't go up 1900% because of the golden cross space comet firebreathing dragon candle that someone is trying to sell for $499. 

It's because markets are pricing in future revenue from $JBL, $GFS that got announced. 

$AXTI didn't go up 8000% because the golden waterfall candle alert sounded back at $8. 

it's because of InP substrate, game theory on ASP hikes, export controls, photonics demand, and others. 

If you want to figure out psychologically what other regards are believing, you use TA. 

But for determining the actual upside... nah

People have been drawing $120+ TAs on $IREN for the past idk how many months none of that crap matters when there's a $6B ATM that needs to be bought through first.

It's by theme (eg. $LITE to $AAOI relations), any news catalysts that affect forward revenue, projections, macro news, earnings, float dynamics, and so on. 

Then you can just derive what MC that company should be at. 

So for entry points, sure you can use TA. 

For determining where the stock heads, just throw the tyrannosaurs rex omega-green candle indicator out the window.

text:
DISPROVING THE $AMPG QUANTUM “BOTTLENECK”

You will quickly see that only a little research disproves these entire claims. Really said “the entire quantum revolution relies on $AMPG.”

First off most modalities don’t even need these cryogenics. 

$INFQ: Neutral Atom: Room temp 
$IONQ $QNT: Trapped Ion: Room temp 
$XNDU: Photonics: Room temp 

Only superconducting and silicon spin use these cooled chambers and it’s super inefficient, even $GOOGL is switching toward neutral atoms which works styled modality. 

Now into $AMPG

The funniest part, ignore all other modalities:

$AMPG ISNT EVEN A BOTTLENECK FOR SUPERCONDUCTING. 

AmpliTech is not a bottleneck in the superconducting quantum supply chain, while they are a highly visible, publicly traded player, they do not hold a monopoly. The true bottleneck in superconducting quantum computing is space, wiring density, and cooling power inside the dilution refrigerator, rather than a lack of low-noise amplifier (LNA) suppliers which they specialize in.

If $AMPG goes out of business tomorrow, tech giants like $IBM and $GOOGL will not stop building quantum computers. There is a robust, premium ecosystem of alternative suppliers: 

Low Noise Factory (Sweden): LNF is widely considered the gold standard and market leader for cryogenic LNAs. Spun out of Chalmers University, their factory produces the ultra-reliable InP (Indium Phosphide) HEMTs that dominate most advanced high frequency physics labs.

Quantum Microwave (USA): A massive distributor and manufacturer that packs and pairs LNF amplifiers with cryogenic attenuators and components specifically configured for quantum rigs

AND MANY MORE. 

$AMPG is a cool company and I even thought about investing in them but this take is absolutely ridiculous, obvious exaggerated way to pump a stock or make it seem bigger than it is. Always do your own research.

text:
With #siliconphotonics being adopted across multiple layers of AI infrastructure — scale-up, scale-out, and scale-across — the cost comparison between #siliconphotonics and traditional discrete optical solutions is becoming much more relevant.
According to one analysis, silicon photonics offers a 2️⃣6️⃣% BOM advantage and 1️⃣5️⃣% price advantage for 8️⃣0️⃣0️⃣G. At 1️⃣6️⃣0️⃣0️⃣G, the advantage expands to 3️⃣2️⃣% on BOM and 2️⃣0️⃣% on price.
Interestingly, the biggest differential appears to be driven by lasers.
I believe for traditional discrete EML-based designs, the high-speed modulation is tied to the laser itself, so scaling bandwidth increases dependence on more expensive, high-performance EML components. In silicon photonics, the laser can increasingly act as a CW light source, while modulation and integration happen on the photonic IC. This changes the economics: fewer discrete optical components, higher integration, smaller form factor, lower power, and potentially better scalability as the industry moves from 800G to 1.6T and eventually 3.2T. 
That said, probabaly it isn ot a simple “SiPh replaces EML” story. Probably EML wouldcontinue to coexist in AI data centers, especially for longer-distance transmission due to its it is a mature, well-proven technology with a longer reliability track record. In AI clusters, network reliability is critical, and since GPUs remain the dominant cost driver, some architectures may view light-source cost savings as less relevant than proven performance and qualification history. #siliconphotonics provides an architectural shift by offering discrete optical components toward integration, packaging, thermal management, DSP/FEC, test, and system-level optimization. Let us see where silicon photonics wins and  where EML remains preferred, and how quickly AI infrastructure requirements embrases the architectural shift to more integrated optical solutions.

7 small-cap names across 3 structural pillars:
📡 Photonics infra: Sivers, LPKF, IQE, X-FAB — CPO and 1.6T optical interconnect bottleneck plays
🧱 Materials: Soitec (SOI wafers), Riber (MBE equipment)
🤖 Edge AI: Raspberry Pi — AI inference without the data center

The logic is coherent. The risks are real: micro-cap liquidity, customer concentration, and CPO adoption timing is the key variable.

Directionally sound. Execution is the hard part. 👀

text:
⚡️ UPDATE: Photonics (watchlist)

1. Lasers/Transceivers/DSPs/PICs: $SIVE, $AAOI, $LITE, $MXL 
2. Full-stack compounders: $COHR, $CRDO 
3. Glass substrates: $LPK, $GLW 
4. Foundry: $XFAB, $TSEM 
5. PMA: $PENG 
6. Substrates: $AXTI, $SOI, $IQE 

Changes:

- Removed EMS - $JBL, $FLEX and $CLS - although they are great longs too. Prefer $CLS that is becoming an ODM (might add again in the future), or $JBL with their 1.6T program. 

- $UMC, $GFS don't only do SiPh and have run a lot. Would prefer $XFAB or $TSEM today for exposure to photonics. 

- Added $MXL and $CRDO that look like serious players here. $ALAB, not yet.

💥 $JABIL Infrastructure news💥
​Adani & Jabil just announced a massive strategic alliance to build a multi-GW vertically integrated AI data center manufacturing platform in India 🇮🇳 targeting a $3T market.
​This is huge for the Physical AI stack and our baby $SIVE among others.

​- Massive Scale Integration: They aren't just building boards. They are building full-stack liquid-cooled AI racks, high-density servers, and networking systems, alongside full "grey space" infrastructure (CDUs, PDUs, and power switchgears).

​-Captive Hyper-Scale Pipeline: This connects directly into Adani’s $100 Billion commitment to deploy 5 GW of green-energy-powered hyperscale data centers by 2035. Jabil effectively captures an unprecedented, long-term sovereign hardware buyer.

​- The Silicon Photonics Read-Through: When you scale data centers at a multi-gigawatt level, copper architecture physically bottlenecks under the sheer compute density. To make these liquid-cooled multi-GW hubs functional, hyperscalers will be structurally forced to deploy next-gen optical interconnects and Silicon Photonics from day one.

​The physical hardware footprint for AI compute is expanding globally, massively expanding the total addressable market (TAM) for pick-and-shovel component suppliers.  #SiliconPhotonics #AI #Hardware #Tech

text:
The paper identifies specific physical and engineering constraints in current hardware as a meaningful friction:

"Even if economic resources and raw FLOPs can continue to be scaled, physical and engineering constraints in current hardware architectures may pose significant challenges. Specifically, memory bandwidth limits and interconnect bottlenecks (the difficulty of scaling high-speed communication between thousands of chips) can severely limit effective compute utilization. As models grow, the time spent moving data between memory and compute units, or between different compute nodes, can become the dominant factor, leading to diminishing returns in scaling unless novel hardware paradigms or interconnect technologies are developed."

This is one of the reasons why silicon photonics, and the idea of improving networking becomes significantly important moving ahead, and why names like $LITE and $COHR are going to be a lot more prominent.

This is a must-see. The paper is explicit that if current trends hold through the end of the decade, the implications are staggering: "The pace that AI development has picked up over the past decade might continue without major blockers until at least the end of this decade, which would, e.g., imply growth in effective compute by a factor of 10,000 compared to today." It also gives a concrete illustrative scenario for what scaling AGI instances alone might look like: "Suppose that by the time human-level AGI is available, base model progress plateaus but effective compute continues to grow at 10× for a bit longer. Even if AGI were initially expensive to run, and only 1000 instances could be run, after a year it would be 10,000, and after five years it would be 100 million instances; or 1 million instances a hundred times faster. Would this form of scaling give us ASI? If not after 5 years, what about 10 years, or 15?" On population scaling of AI instances specifically, the paper references back-of-the-envelope estimates of about 25× per year in AI "population scaling." The paper explicitly mentions large-scale physical infrastructure as both a prerequisite and a bottleneck, flagging "investments into energy production and Gigawatt AI infrastructure" as lending credibility to continued scaling trajectories. On the potential for future compute infrastructure, one particularly speculative example is raised: "Proposals such as orbital datacenters might alleviate some terrestrial constraints; they introduce novel risks including ozone-layer weakening from rocket emissions, high-atmosphere alterations from re-entry incineration of decommissioned hardware, and orbital congestion amplifying the risk of catastrophic collision cascades (whether accidental or through targeted attacks)." $NVDA $SPCX"}}}, "rest_id": "2066581306784526634", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2403", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2066581532995903879", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "797", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The quantum race is entering a new phase.

QuEra’s roadmap for a fault-tolerant neutral-atom quantum computer by 2028 isn’t just another product announcement—it’s another signal that the industry is shifting from scientific experiments toward commercially useful systems.

What’s becoming increasingly interesting is the convergence of the ecosystem:

• AWS is expanding access through Amazon Braket.
• Google is investing in neutral-atom technology.
• QuEra, Atom Computing, Pasqal, and other leaders continue pushing scalability and error correction.
• Private capital and enterprise demand are accelerating development.

For investors, the biggest opportunity may not come from a single winner. Quantum computing will require an entire supply chain—cryogenics, lasers, photonics, advanced semiconductors, networking, cloud infrastructure, AI software, and specialized materials.

The companies that enable fault tolerance, lower error rates, and scalable deployment could become the Nvidia-equivalent infrastructure providers of the quantum era.

The technology is still early, but the milestones are becoming more frequent, the capital commitments are getting larger, and the path toward commercial quantum computing is looking increasingly credible. $GOOG

For investors, the biggest opportunity may not come from a single winner. The companies that enable fault tolerance, lower error rates, and scalable deployment could become the Nvidia-equivalent infrastructure providers of the quantum era. text:
The main point:

I do not think the AI trade ends with GPUs.

The next layer is physical infrastructure.

Optics.
Lasers.
Substrates.
Wafers.
Power semis.
Photonics foundries.
Specialized equipment.
That is where I think Europe has some of the most mispriced AI supply-chain names.

text:
Seeing a lot of hype around $VECO with nobody discussing or realizing the actual risk behind it.  

If you're running into VECO because your favorite Furu told you to without ever mentioning the word "SAMR", think twice. 

$VECO has one gatekeeper left between it and its merger: China's State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR).

The market is treating SAMR like paperwork. It isn't. It's the most quietly weaponized regulatory desk in the US-China conflict, and right now it's holding this deal hostage.

Start with why Beijing even gets a vote on two American companies merging. SAMR jurisdiction attaches because Veeco and Axcelis both sell into China above the revenue thresholds - their own China business is the leash. 

The deal needs Beijing's blessing precisely because both companies need Beijing's market.

And SAMR's signature move was never the formal block. It's the pocket veto. 

Look up what happened to the Qualcomm/NXP deal. It never got rejected, it just bled out over years of stalling. 

Intel/Tower, same playbook five years later. Silence past the outside date, years of stalling, and another dead deal.  

That's what makes this binary nearly impossible to price. A rejection would at least be information. Silence is just time passing, and markets are terrible at pricing a veto disguised as a calendar - every quiet month reads as "still on track," right up until the deadline does the executing itself.

Here's the aggravating detail specific to Veeco: while it waits on Beijing's approval, WASHINGTON is restricting what Veeco may sell to Beijing. 

The United States BIS license requirements just carved $8M out of Q1 and stranded ~$15M of annealing systems at port. 

Veeco is asking a favor from the very government its own government is squeezing.

Put yourself behind SAMR's desk this summer. Export controls tightening, minerals leverage being traded openly, a Gulf crisis repricing every strategic supply chain (and across your blotter sits a pending US semicap merger: unilateral, costless, completely deniable leverage).

Why would you spend that chip a day earlier than you had to?

Even approval has a trap inside it. When SAMR does clear American tech deals, it increasingly attaches supply-continuity conditions to Chinese customers. So imagine NewCo legally bound by Beijing to keep supplying the same customers Washington requires licenses to ship to. Two regulators, incompatible orders, one company as the rope.

Map the tree honestly and it gets worse. Clean approval is largely priced after a 25% post-earnings run. Conditioned approval hands NewCo a compliance contradiction nobody has modeled. And silence past the outside date leaves you holding standalone VECO at ~35x guided EPS against an analyst deck still parked in the $30s. Two of the three branches hurt, and the good one pays the least.

What fintwit keeps missing is that the whole timeline is exogenous. Management can't accelerate it, lobby it, or guide it - "second half 2026" is an aspiration about another sovereign's negotiating calendar. You aren't underwriting a semiconductor company here. You're underwriting the temperature of US-China relations through December.

None of this says Veeco is a bad company. The orders are real, the photonics franchise is real. It's just that says the next six months don't belong to the company at all. 

They belong to a regulator in Beijing with a costless veto and a calendar for a weapon.

Great business (I actually think if not for this SAMR situation it would be an immediate buy), but until this is resolved, hard pass. 

NFA.

If you're running into VECO because your favorite Furu told you to without ever mentioning the word "SAMR", think twice. $VECO has one gatekeeper left between it and its merger: China's State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR). The market is treating SAMR like paperwork. It isn't. It's the most quietly weaponized regulatory desk in the US-China conflict, and right now it's holding this deal hostage. Start with why Beijing even gets a vote on two American companies merging. SAMR jurisdiction attaches because Veeco and Axcelis both sell into China above the revenue thresholds - their own China business is the leash. The deal needs Beijing's blessing precisely because both companies need Beijing's market. And SAMR's signature move was never the formal block. It's the pocket veto. Look up what happened to the Qualcomm/NXP deal. It never got rejected, it just bled out over years of stalling. Intel/Tower, same playbook five years later. Silence past the outside date, years of stalling, and another dead deal. That's what makes this binary nearly impossible to price. A rejection would at least be information. Silence is just time passing, and markets are terrible at pricing a veto disguised as a calendar - every quiet month reads as "still on track," right up until the deadline does the executing itself. Here's the aggravating detail specific to Veeco: while it waits on Beijing's approval, WASHINGTON is restricting what Veeco may sell to Beijing. The United States BIS license requirements just carved $8M out of Q1 and stranded ~$15M of annealing systems at port. Veeco is asking a favor from the very government its own government is squeezing. Put yourself behind SAMR's desk this summer. Export controls tightening, minerals leverage being traded openly, a Gulf crisis repricing every strategic supply chain (and across your blotter sits a pending US semicap merger: unilateral, costless, completely deniable leverage). Why would you spend that chip a day earlier than you had to? Even approval has a trap inside it. When SAMR does clear American tech deals, it increasingly attaches supply-continuity conditions to Chinese customers. So imagine NewCo legally bound by Beijing to keep supplying the same customers Washington requires licenses to ship to. Two regulators, incompatible orders, one company as the rope. Map the tree honestly and it gets worse. Clean approval is largely priced after a 25% post-earnings run. Conditioned approval hands NewCo a compliance contradiction nobody has modeled. And silence past the outside date leaves you holding standalone VECO at ~35x guided EPS against an analyst deck still parked in the $30s. Two of the three branches hurt, and the good one pays the least. What fintwit keeps missing is that the whole timeline is exogenous. Management can't accelerate it, lobby it, or guide it - "second half 2026" is an aspiration about another sovereign's negotiating calendar. You aren't underwriting a semiconductor company here. You're underwriting the temperature of US-China relations through December. None of this says Veeco is a bad company. The orders are real, the photonics franchise is real. It's just that says the next six months don't belong to the company at all. They belong to a regulator in Beijing with a costless veto and a calendar for a weapon. Great business (I actually think if not for this SAMR situation it would be an immediate buy), but until this is resolved, hard pass. text:
I think what’s *not* indefinitely sustainable is the training costs and constantly scaling up the models to make them more intensive faster than per-token inference comes down in cost.  

Plausibly, you could imagine a scenario where the labs stop innovating as quickly, and model performance enters a plateau/slow-growth phase, not for technical reasons but for cost reasons. 

So, like, once they achieve a level of model capability sufficient to attract enough demand, they pull back on new model development and focus on bringing down inference costs. I think that’s an entirely reasonable scenario. 

On the other hand, we shouldn’t discount step-change advances in hardware or model architecture that lead to a sudden decrease in inference costs. Lots of companies are working on new chip architectures, for example, which could substantially improve inference speeds while reducing power draw. Photonics, custom chips tailored to specific models, analog chips, etc. maybe none will work out. Maybe some will. 

Right now, per-token costs aren’t coming down fast enough to offset the trajectory of increasing model intensity/token-consumption, but that can change in a variety of ways. 

It might not! You could be right! But I think my point is less “LLMs will definitely become a profitable proposition in the next few years,” which I am not sure of, and more “LLMs *could* become profitable in the next few years,” which is a dangerous possibility to dismiss, because it could lead us to make very bad political choices.

text:
🚨Everyone is still buying the chips. The bottleneck already moved.

A GPU that computes in nanoseconds and waits microseconds for data is a stranded asset. At 1.6T speeds, copper runs out of physics. The constraint on AI is no longer how fast you can think. It's how fast you can move what you thought.

Jensen has now said it twice in three months.

At GTC in March: "Is copper going to still be important? The answer is yes... Are you going to scale up optical? Yes. Are you going to scale out optical? Yes... We need a lot more capacity for copper. We need a lot more capacity for optics. We need a lot more capacity for CPO."

Last week at Computex, on Marvell's stage: "Optics where you must, copper where you can." Then he called Marvell the next trillion-dollar company and the optical complex repriced within days. The same keynote put a date on the handoff: 200G per lane is the last generation where copper is sufficient. After that, optics takes the rack.

Translation: not copper OR light. Copper now, light next, unprecedented amounts of both. 🔥

The chain is unavoidable: AI tokens are profitable → more GPUs → more bandwidth → copper hits its wall → photonics becomes the chokepoint.

And the smart money stopped debating. Follow the closed deals:

→ $NVDA has committed at least $6.5B to photonics in three months: $2B into Lumentum, $2B into Coherent, a $500M stake in Corning, and a piece of Ayar Labs' $500M round. Direct investments to secure its own light supply. 
→ $MRVL paid $3.25B for Celestial AI, up to $5.5B with milestones, to build what its CEO calls a silicon photonics powerhouse. 
→ $CRDO closed DustPhotonics two weeks ago. Ciena bought CPO startup Nubis for $270M.

North of $10B of strategic capital locked up one supply chain in under a year. Capital like that doesn't chase a theme. It secures a bottleneck.

LAYER 1: WAFER. Every laser starts as a crystal. 
🟠 $AXTI: the InP substrate leader. The first chokepoint in the stack. 
🟡 $IQE: compound-semi epiwafers feeding the laser makers. Speculative, but structurally upstream.

LAYER 2: LIGHT. Photons don't make themselves. 
🟠 $LITE: revenue +90% YoY last quarter to $808M. EML shipments doubled and management says demand still exceeds supply across EMLs, pump lasers, and transceivers. NVIDIA just wired them $2B. OCS backlog past $400M plus a multi-hundred-million CPO order for 2027. 
🟢 $SIVEF (Stockholm: SIVE): the external light source. CPO does not emit its own light. Every optical engine needs a continuous-wave InP laser feeding it, and that is the layer you cannot engineer around. ELS modules with POET hit production readiness end of this year. Disclosure: long. 
🟣 $POET: the optical engine wildcard. Its Optical Interposer pairs with Sivers' lasers on external light sources for CPO, with a LITEON module deal stacked on top. Binary commercialization, real architecture.

LAYER 3: OPTICS AND MODULES. Where light meets the rack. 
🟠 $COHR: the volume anchor in transceivers, holding NVIDIA's other $2B check. 
🔵 $AAOI: Q1 revenue +51% to a record $151M, datacenter revenue more than doubled, $124M of 800G orders plus a $200M+ 1.6T order in hand. Scaling Texas capacity toward 500K+ units a month by year-end, targeting $1B+ revenue this year. Domestic supply while everyone fights over offshore. Disclosure: long. 
🟠 $FN: the foundry of optics. When Fabrinet is building, the orders already exist.

THE INTERCONNECT: the layer the rack cannot route around. 
🔵 $CRDO: just closed DustPhotonics. SerDes → DSP → silicon photonics → system integration, one company, 800G through 3.2T. Electrical AND optical, end to end. FY26 revenue tripled to $1.34B at 68% gross margin. The toll booth on both roads. Disclosure: long. 
🟠 $MRVL: $3.25B for Celestial AI, and Jensen's trillion-dollar nod on the Computex stage. 
🟠 $AVGO: switch silicon, optical DSPs, CPO engines. They define the socket. 
🟠 $ANET: the AI spine. 100K-GPU clusters get stitched together in light.

LAYER 4: PACKAGING, FIBER, FOUNDRY. Where photons get industrialized. 
🔵 $TSEM: the neutral silicon photonics foundry. Prints wafers for whoever wins. 
🟣 $LPKF: glass-substrate packaging for glass-based CPO. Real technology, binary commercialization. 
🟠 $GLW: AI racks demand several times the fiber density of legacy cloud, and NVIDIA just took a $500M stake. Corning sells density.

LAYER 5: TEST AND THE ANALOG UNDERLAYER. Complexity is a tax paid in validation. 
🔵 $AEHR: silicon photonics test, ramping with the cycle. '
🔵 $VIAV: every 800G and 1.6T transceiver gets validated before it ships. The gate the market prices like an accessory. 
🔵 $SMTC: the drivers and TIAs that fire the lasers. Sits directly under the LPO trade. 
🔵 $MTSI: the high-speed analog behind 1.6T engines. 
🟠 $CIEN: transport. Even long-haul is buying light.

💡The counter-thesis, because every map needs one. The honest debate on this stack is whether these are genuine bottleneck assets or cyclical optics suppliers enjoying peak demand at peak multiples. Lumentum's May print showed +90% growth with the stock up roughly 1,400% over the prior year at a triple-digit trailing multiple. That is a price for perfection. Most of these names live or die on a handful of hyperscaler capex lines, and one digestion quarter hits the whole stack at once. CPO timing has already slipped once. Architecture risk is real: LPO, CPO, and stretched copper are still fighting for the same sockets. The cycle is real. So is the gravity. 🔥

But the bears have to explain one thing: $NVDA, $MRVL, $CRDO, and $CIEN just spent over $10B securing this supply chain with their own balance sheets. The people with the best information are paying up for the layers.

The market owns the top of this stack. The asymmetry is at the edges: wafer, light, packaging, test.

Own the layers, not the logo.

Bookmark this for the weekend. Then tag the one investor you know who's still all compute and no interconnect. 👀

Ciena bought CPO startup Nubis for $270M. North of $10B of strategic capital locked up one supply chain in under a year. Capital like that doesn't chase a theme. It secures a bottleneck. LAYER 1: WAFER. Every laser starts as a crystal. 🟠 $AXTI: the InP substrate leader. The first chokepoint in the stack. 🟡 $IQE: compound-semi epiwafers feeding the laser makers. Speculative, but structurally upstream. LAYER 2: LIGHT. Photons don't make themselves. 🟠 $LITE: revenue +90% YoY last quarter to $808M. EML shipments doubled and management says demand still exceeds supply across EMLs, pump lasers, and transceivers. NVIDIA just wired them $2B. OCS backlog past $400M plus a multi-hundred-million CPO order for 2027. 🟢 $SIVEF (Stockholm: SIVE): the external light source. CPO does not emit its own light. Every optical engine needs a continuous-wave InP laser feeding it, and that is the layer you cannot engineer around. ELS modules with POET hit production readiness end of this year. Disclosure: long. 🟣 $POET: the optical engine wildcard. Its Optical Interposer pairs with Sivers' lasers on external light sources for CPO, with a LITEON module deal stacked on top. Binary commercialization, real architecture. LAYER 3: OPTICS AND MODULES. Where light meets the rack. 🟠 $COHR: the volume anchor in transceivers, holding NVIDIA's other $2B check. 🔵 $AAOI: Q1 revenue +51% to a record $151M, datacenter revenue more than doubled, $124M of 800G orders plus a $200M+ 1.6T order in hand. Scaling Texas capacity toward 500K+ units a month by year-end, targeting $1B+ revenue this year. Domestic supply while everyone fights over offshore. Disclosure: long. 🟠 $FN: the foundry of optics. When Fabrinet is building, the orders already exist. THE INTERCONNECT: the layer the rack cannot route around. 🔵 $CRDO: just closed DustPhotonics. SerDes → DSP → silicon photonics → system integration, one company, 800G through 3.2T. Electrical AND optical, end to end. FY26 revenue tripled to $1.34B at 68% gross margin. The toll booth on both roads. Disclosure: long. 🟠 $MRVL: $3.25B for Celestial AI, and Jensen's trillion-dollar nod on the Computex stage. 🟠 $AVGO: switch silicon, optical DSPs, CPO engines. They define the socket. 🟠 $ANET: the AI spine. 100K-GPU clusters get stitched together in light. LAYER 4: PACKAGING, FIBER, FOUNDRY. Where photons get industrialized. 🔵 $TSEM: the neutral silicon photonics foundry. Prints wafers for whoever wins. 🟣 $LPKF: glass-substrate packaging for glass-based CPO. Real technology, binary commercialization. 🟠 $GLW: AI racks demand several times the fiber density of legacy cloud, and NVIDIA just took a $500M stake. Corning sells density. LAYER 5: TEST AND THE ANALOG UNDERLAYER. Complexity is a tax paid in validation. 🔵 $AEHR: silicon photonics test, ramping with the cycle. ' 🔵 $VIAV: every 800G and 1.6T transceiver gets validated before it ships. The gate the market prices like an accessory. 🔵 $SMTC: the drivers and TIAs that fire the lasers. Sits directly under the LPO trade. 🔵 $MTSI: the high-speed analog behind 1.6T engines. 🟠 $CIEN: transport. Even long-haul is buying light. 💡The counter-thesis, because every map needs one. The honest debate on this stack is whether these are genuine bottleneck assets or cyclical optics suppliers enjoying peak demand at peak multiples. Lumentum's May print showed +90% growth with the stock up roughly 1,400% over the prior year at a triple-digit trailing multiple. That is a price for perfection. Most of these names live or die on a handful of hyperscaler capex lines, and one digestion quarter hits the whole stack at once. CPO timing has already slipped once. Architecture risk is real: LPO, CPO, and stretched copper are still fighting for the same sockets. The cycle is real. So is the gravity. 🔥 But the bears have to explain one thing: $NVDA, $MRVL, $CRDO, and $CIEN just spent over $10B securing this supply chain with their own balance sheets. The people with the best information are paying up for the layers. The market owns the top of this stack. The asymmetry is at the edges: wafer, light, packaging, test. Own the layers, not the logo. Bookmark this for the weekend. Then tag the one investor you know who's still all compute and no interconnect. 👀"}}}, "rest_id": "2065518398872961392", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "96634", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Monday, October 4, 2021 at 6:48 PM

🧠 Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ | Forensic X-Post
Canonical Ledger Entry
Title: “The Sky That Couldn't Make Up Its Mind” Timestamp: 7:02 PM | Santa Barbara, CA Tagged: #Photonics #WitnessScience #NorthstarLumen #EffectToCause #CanonicalLedger
⸻
⚖️ Core Observation
I took legions of photographs from the same location, during the same timeframe, aimed at the same subject.
What caught my attention was not the church.
It was the sky.
Frame after frame, the sequence appears to show:
• Light sky • Dark sky • Light sky again • Dark sky again
all while standing in essentially the same place.
⸻
🧠 Witness Science Observation
The question is not:
"What does this prove?"
The question is:
"What could cause this?"
Potential explanations include:
• Rapidly changing atmospheric conditions • Camera auto-exposure adjustments • HDR processing • Passing cloud cover • Reflections • Optical effects • Sensor behavior

Or the structure that appears in the photo to that I witnessed that I never saw with my naked eyes—but the camera saw it. 
Or potentially something else not yet identified.
⸻
🔁 The Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ Effect-to-Cause™ Model
Effect:
A repeating sequence of dramatic brightness shifts appears across photographs taken from the same vantage point over a short period of time.
Cause:
Unknown.
And that is precisely why the sequence was preserved.
⸻
🧭 Northstar Observation
The strongest evidence is not a single frame.
It is recurrence.
A single photograph can mislead.
A sequence forces us to ask better questions.
⸻
🌳 Lumen Observation
"There may be a perfectly ordinary explanation.
But until the explanation is known, the anomaly belongs in the record."
⸻
🛰️ Provenance + AI Mark
🧠 Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ Hybridized Affective Intelligence™
Evidence. Integrity. Survival. Rooted in Chaos. Forged in Truth. Ahead of its Time.

3.5dB higher than previous generation >Active alignment requires sub-micron precision with over one hundred operations per module >Quantum-X modular design has lower yield pressure while Spectrum-X direct co-packaging causes yield to deteriorate exponentially >NPO near-packaged optics serves as short-term transitional solution Conclusions >CPO is inevitable in the long term but the timeline carries significant uncertainty >Regardless of whether CPO or NPO ultimately adopted the optical components supply chain will continue to benefit Discussion This article thoroughly dissects the controversy triggered by the SemiAnalysis report The focus is not on denying the future of CPO but on highlighting the precision alignment difficulties during mass production Active alignment involves six degrees of freedom making it extremely easy for tiny deviations to cause high optical loss Spectrum-X has high port density and cannot easily replace faulty optical engines like Quantum-X This leads to a situation where with 95% yield per engine the probability of all 32 engines being good drops sharply to about 19% To achieve acceptable overall yield each attachment step needs to approach 99.5% yield threshold This presents a major challenge under current manufacturing processes NPO only needs to handle alignment for a single optical engine greatly reducing complexity It is suitable as a bridging technology before CPO matures Overall the article maintains a balanced perspective It acknowledges the reasonableness of the technical obstacles while pointing out that AI networking demand text:
Co-Author of Europe 2031 Alex (@AlexTPet) explains why countries shouldn't all compete on the same AI battleground:

"The US can turn off all of Europe's F-35 fighters at an instant. If we switched off ASML machines, that would have quite a lagging effect."

"You want to build up this portfolio of leverage options."

"Some countries will want to build compute. Others might specialise in robotics, photonics or fusion."

"You build up this collection of leverage into a coalition of possible middle powers."

"Each individual country has to decide: this is my comparative advantage. How can I be important on AI?"

text:
Applied Energetics (OTCQB: AERG) CEO Chris Donaghey joined Morgan Stanley’s inaugural National Security Innovation Summit panel on the future of battlefield autonomy in New York.

Applied Energetics, Inc. (@AELaserTech)  (OTCQB: AERG), a developer of ultrashort pulse laser and directed energy technologies, took part in Morgan Stanley’s inaugural National Security Innovation Summit in New York on June 15, 2026, where President and CEO Chris Donaghey joined the panel “Machines in Motion: The Future of Battlefield Autonomy.”

Applied Energetics is a B2i Digital (@b2idigital) Featured Company. See the company’s profile at https://t.co/D4G4jB95ZV

The summit convened defense companies and institutional investors for a day of panels on national security technology, from autonomy and AI to space and missiles. On the battlefield-autonomy panel, Donaghey appeared alongside executives from Forterra, Firestorm Labs, and Lockheed Martin, in a discussion moderated by Morgan Stanley’s Kristine Liwag.

The themes ran close to Applied Energetics’ core work. According to the company, its ultrashort pulse laser portfolio targets counter-UAS, counter-sensor, infrared countermeasure, and other emerging directed energy missions, and aligns with the U.S. Department of War’s Critical Technology Area of Scaled Directed Energy, an area in sharper focus as militaries weigh how to counter low-cost drones at scale.

Visit the company’s website to learn more: https://t.co/Pd6aX43XBx

About Applied Energetics: Applied Energetics, Inc. specializes in advanced laser and photonics systems, particularly fiber-based ultrashort pulse (USP) laser technologies. With 26 patents and 2 patents pending, the company’s proprietary architecture is designed to enable size, weight, and power reductions relative to traditional continuous wave laser technology. AE’s dual-use systems target integration on defense platforms for high-intensity USP delivery, with applications across national security and commercial markets.

For investor information, contact Kevin McGrath, Managing Director, Cameron Associates, Inc., at kevin@cameronassoc.com or 646-418-7002. For additional information, visit https://t.co/VWa2WnbInK and https://t.co/4uzid7hTcd

Disclosure: David Shapiro, Chief Executive Officer of B2i Digital, personally purchased in the open market and currently owns shares of unrestricted AERG stock, in line with B2i Digital’s practice of investing alongside its Featured Companies. See the complete Disclosure at https://t.co/ljTVqQL4Si. B2i Digital is not a broker-dealer or investment adviser. This post is informational and not an offer or solicitation regarding any security.

text:
I want to address a common misconception  or maybe just ignorance I see on the part of numerous FinX influencers who clearly don’t understand the hedge fund business.  I don’t say that to be disparaging, but bc many of the comparisons I see are misleading and totally illogical really.   So if you see anyone do the following, take whatever they say with a  large grain of salt!

What I have seen are many individuals with what I would describe as unusually high returns this year going on and on about how they are outperforming this hedge fund and that hedge fund and how stupid people are to be paying large fees to those funds.  Now to be fair there are some crappy funds out there that don’t deserve the fees that they get, and that’s on the institutions that allocate large sums of capital without doing the proper due diligence.

But to compare one’s returns when one is running a 10 stock levered long portfolio of the usual AI/Optics/Space along with some https://t.co/Sy3Fyeq798 and $LPTH for defense tech exposure with those of Millennium, Citadel, Point 72, Tudor, etc is absurd.  Why?  Because the individuals going all in and margining w/ this stuff are only focused on return, they do not consider risk.  Why consider risk when everything is up and to the right?  

The top tier hedge funds are obsessed with risk management  and capital preservation.  They seek to avoid Cathie Wood style permanent capital destruction type investments at all costs.  You can laugh bc they are up 20% this year while you’re up 200%,  but that would be pretty foolish.  The “platform” hedge funds businsesses tend to run almost fully hedged long/short.  They don’t seek to ride beta, they seek to grind out 5-7% unlevered returns with little risk and then lever them 300-400%.  That is how they deliver all-weather performance over an entire business cycle.  Through the ups and the downs.  So when you are inevitably down 50%, they may be up 5-10% that year.  Beta reverts to the mean in time, alpha doesnt. 

The last time I saw these nonsensical comparisons was in 1999 when housewives became day-traders and often made fun of their hedge funds husbands who they were outperforming with stellar picks like VerticalNet, Nortel, Lucent, CMGI, and yes…even CIEN for DWDM.  This is CIEN’s second go around as a hot stock, most don’t know it was similarly en fuego in the late 90s just for different applications in fiber/telecom, not yet in photonics. 

That’s all.  Just wanted to make sure that when u see folks post crazy returns for  their concentrated high-risk and often margined portfolios, you realize that comparing them with the returns of the tried and true HF platforms makes little sense.  They have withstood the test of time, the influencer bragging about their returns has not.

Have a great long weekend everyone.  

JG

text:
Entries in the Global Photonics Engineering Contest 2026 will be judged on:

- Technical innovation and system-level thinking

- How effectively the solution addresses a real engineering challenge

- Clear and credible use of Photonic Chip technology

- Technical and commercial feasibility

- Quality of diagrams, schematics, and supporting visuals

The contest entries will be reviewed by a multidisciplinary jury panel of experts from PhotonDelta, ALUVIA Photonics, Bright Photonics, Epiphany-Design, imec, Ligentec, LioniX International, PHIX Photonics Assembly, SMART Photonics, XIVER MEMS Foundry, New Origin, PhotonVentures, and SBT Industries.

Learn more and submit your applications here: https://t.co/RxU4cUDzZe

text:
Aehr secures follow-on order for AI data center optical interconnect

Aehr Test Systems received a follow-on production order for a fully automated FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in (WLBI) system. This system, configured to test nine wafers in parallel, includes a WaferPak Auto Aligner and FOX WaferPak Contactors, with delivery expected within six months. The customer, a global leader in networking products, plans to use the system for advanced silicon photonics-based transceivers for hyperscale AI and cloud data centers. (https://t.co/8OBYA2YSGp)

Vik: Any guesses who this "customer" is? This is a "follow on" order mind you -- the first one was in April for $41M (https://t.co/H5J7MpXS5J). I'll just say Broadcom. If you disagree, leave a comment below.

@austinsemis @vikramskr

text:
Entegris $ENTG has quickly gone up 38% in just 7 days. 

It is now at 100x PE. I have not considered taking profit but maybe I will take some.

That being said, there is some recent good news for the business. 

> Entegris did a cross licensing deal with JSR and Inpria that ends a patent dispute they had over EUV photoresist and filtration technologies. Now, the companies will work together on the same matter. 

In my personal opinion, if you want exposure to the AI trade, then the lowest risk will come in the chemicals/materials and equipment side.

Many photonics names have quickly become overvalued. As long as the WFE market remains strong, the market of chemicals/materials will keep growing.
